element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Primary vehicle throughout; player learns machine gun, Hydra rockets, Hellfire missiles progression
Baseline weapon; infinite ammo but lowest damage
More powerful than machine guns; limited carrying capacity introduces resource management
Most deadly weapon; severely limited ammo forces tactical weapon selection
Continuous resource drain; fuel barrels provide relief; forces map navigation awareness
Depletes from enemy fire; replenished by power-ups or airlifting friendlies; zero armor = loss of life
Limited ammunition supply; players must decide weapon load-out strategically
Secondary objectives; rescues provide armor replenishment; landing zone requirement
Stationary threats; players learn to outmaneuver and prioritize targets
Alternative vehicle; presumably faster/more agile than helicopter
Alternative vehicle for specific terrain/mission types
Variable height capability; unlimited ammunition; high crash vulnerability; risk/reward tradeoff
Light enemy vehicles; escalating threat complexity
Heavy enemy installations; higher threat level; weapon selection becomes critical
Mission objective variant; different from rescue mechanics
Boss-equivalent threat; four targets requiring elimination before reaching White House

element_introduction_order.first_appears: Opening sequence; pursued throughout; final confrontation Level N
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Penultimate level: player pursues to hideouts for capture; final level: elimination

typical_tension_triggers: Mission objective timer (implied hostage rescue urgency)
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Cyclical tension between resource scarcity (fuel, ammo, armor) and combat encounters; relief comes from collectibles and mission objectives completion; escalating enemy threat complexity mirrors resource management difficulty

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Hostage/POW locations and enemy installations distributed across level map; player chooses engagement priority

difficulty_curve.early_game: Washington D.C. defense level introduces core mechanics (helicopter control, basic weapons, resource management, enemy patterns); moderate threat level with multiple mission objectives
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Jungle fortress counter-attacks escalate enemy variety (armored cars to tanks/artillery); vehicle switching introduces new handling/vulnerability tradeoffs; hostage rescues and enemy capture missions add strategic layer
difficulty_curve.late_game: Penultimate level: high-stakes antagonist pursuit to hideouts; Final level: return to Washington D.C. with nuclear bomb truck threat (four targets) plus antagonist elimination; Alaska extension (PC): ecosystem threat under Russian defector; sustained high threat
notable_spikes: F-117 stealth jet introduction (high crash vulnerability vs. unlimited ammo)
Final level: simultaneous management of four nuclear bomb trucks + antagonist elimination
Alaska level (PC version): new adversary type and environmental stakes

notable_level_patterns.description: Each level features steadily depleting fuel forcing continuous map traversal; ammo/armor management requires strategic weapon selection and hostage rescue prioritization for replenishment
Level 1 introduces protagonist defending capital from terrorist attack; Final level returns player to D.C. for ultimate confrontation with antagonists and nuclear threat
Levels based on destruction of weapons/installations, hostage rescue, and/or personnel capture; nonlinear mission sequencing allows player tactical choice
Player commandeers motorbike, hovercraft, or F-117 based on mission requirements; each vehicle has distinct tradeoffs (speed vs. vulnerability, ammo vs. crash risk)
Kilbaba and Ortega introduced in opening; pursued throughout jungle campaign; penultimate level forces direct pursuit to hideouts; final level forces elimination
